**Summary of what I found and why:**

**P2 — isDragging=false dead zone (confidence: 100).** Every test in `useTouchDrag.test.ts` issues an `onPointerMove` that crosses `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX=8` before calling `onPointerUp`, ensuring `isDragging.current=true`. The forgiveness block at `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:296` is inside `if (isDragging.current)`. A genuine fast tap — pointer down, immediately up, never moved 8px — bypasses the entire forgiveness path and `onTapFallback` will never fire. This is not a style gap; it is a functional coverage gap for a real device scenario.

**P2 — Inverse guard combination untested (confidence: 100).** The guard is a two-part `&&`: `tapForgivenessThreshold !== undefined && onTapFallbackRef.current`. Test 4 exercises threshold set + no callback. The mirror case — callback provided + no threshold — is untested. The API allows it and the silent no-op in that branch is a latent trap for future callers.

**P3 — Missing negative assertion on speakTile in onTapFallback test (confidence: 75).** The `onTapFallback` implementation in `useDraggableTile.ts:144-147` deliberately omits `speakTile` (unlike `handleClick`). The test at line 266 of `useDraggableTile.test.tsx` asserts the affirmative side (dispatch + placeInNextSlot) but does not assert `mockSpeakTile` was not called. This leaves no regression guard if someone later adds `speakTile` to the callback.

**P3 — No default-value test for tapForgivenessThreshold in useSettings (confidence: 75).** `useSettings.test.tsx` tests the default for `volume` (0.8) as a pattern but the new `tapForgivenessThreshold: 20` default added to `DEFAULT_SETTINGS` has no equivalent test.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-05-02 03:32:49Z)**_

I now have everything I need to produce the full review. Let me compile the findings.

---

## Agent-Native Architecture Review

### Summary

BaseSkill is a local-first educational game app (React + TanStack Router + RxDB/IndexedDB). Agent integration is provided entirely through the Playwright MCP server configured in `.mcp.json` -- there are no in-process tool definitions (no `streamText`, no `tool()` registrations, no OpenAI/Anthropic SDK tool calls in the source tree). The Playwright MCP means an agent can drive the browser UI but cannot operate on the data layer directly. The tap forgiveness PR adds one new user-facing setting (`tapForgivenessThreshold`) accessible only through the Settings screen slider. The review below evaluates whether an agent retains parity with that new capability.

---

### Capability Map

| UI Action | Location | Agent Tool | In Prompt? | Priority | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drag slider to set tap forgiveness threshold (8-60 px) | `src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx:136-145` | Playwright MCP `mouse_move` + `mouse_click` (browser automation) | No | Must Have | Partial -- see Finding 1 |
| Read current tapForgivenessThreshold value | `useSettings()` hook / RxDB `settings` doc | No direct DB read; requires navigating to settings page via Playwright | No | Must Have | Gap -- see Finding 2 |
| Programmatically patch any settings field (volume, speechRate, ttsEnabled, showSubtitles, preferredVoiceURI) | `useSettings().update()` | None -- same gap applies to all pre-existing settings fields | No | Must Have | Pre-existing gap |

---

### Findings

#### Critical (Must Fix)

None that are newly introduced by this PR. The structural gap (no programmatic settings write path for agents) pre-dates this PR and is described as a Warning below.

#### Warnings (Should Fix)

**1. No programmatic write path for tapForgivenessThreshold -- agent must drive a slider via Playwright**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx:136-145` and `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.ts:59-75`

The only agent-accessible path to set `tapForgivenessThreshold` is to navigate the Playwright MCP to `/$locale/settings`, locate the slider, and perform pointer interactions. This is fragile: the slider uses `onValueChange` fired by pointer events, so keyboard or direct value injection via `input` field attributes will not trigger the RxDB write. The `useSettings().update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: v })` function is a clean primitive that already accepts a numeric patch -- it is not exposed in any server-side action, CLI script, or agent-callable endpoint.

This gap is not new (all other settings fields have the same problem), but this PR extends the affected surface by one field without improving parity.

Recommendation: Expose a thin Node.js script (similar to the existing `scripts/dev/dump-db.js` pattern visible in the root git status) or a dedicated RxDB seed/patch utility that accepts JSON patches and writes them directly to the IndexedDB storage file. Alternatively, add an `update-settings` Playwright MCP helper that takes a key-value argument and calls the hook programmatically via browser `evaluate`, removing the fragile slider interaction requirement.

**2. tapForgivenessThreshold is absent from the useSettings demo and any agent-visible surface that describes available settings fields**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.demo.tsx:10-16`

The `UseSettingsDemo` component that is rendered in Storybook lists `volume`, `speechRate`, `ttsEnabled`, and `showSubtitles` but does not render `tapForgivenessThreshold`. An agent using Storybook MCP to understand the settings schema would miss the new field entirely. The demo is the de facto living documentation for what settings exist.

Recommendation: Add a `tapForgivenessThreshold` display line to `useSettings.demo.tsx` to match the other fields.

#### Observations

**3. DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX (8 px, hardcoded) and tapForgivenessThreshold serve different purposes but the relationship is not documented**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:4` and `:197-200`

`DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX` is the minimum displacement to initiate a drag; `tapForgivenessThreshold` is the maximum displacement at pointer-up to retroactively classify a completed drag as a tap. Both are in pixel units, the schema minimum for `tapForgivenessThreshold` is 8 (matching `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX`), but that constraint relationship is implicit. An agent asked to reason about tuning touch sensitivity has no context to know that setting `tapForgivenessThreshold` below `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX` would create a dead zone where a gesture is neither a tap nor a recognised drag. A code comment co-located with the schema constraint, or a validation note in the settings hook, would give an agent enough context to avoid recommending an invalid configuration.

**4. The useSettings unit test suite does not include a test for tapForgivenessThreshold default or persistence**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.test.tsx`

The test file covers `volume` and `speechRate` but not the new field. The `useDraggableTile` and `useTouchDrag` test files do exercise the threshold at the integration level, but a unit test confirming that `useSettings` returns `20` as the default for `tapForgivenessThreshold` and that `update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: 40 })` persists correctly would complete coverage consistent with the existing test pattern and satisfy the TDD requirement stated in CLAUDE.md.

**5. Playwright MCP is the only registered MCP server in `.mcp.json`; the settings collection has no agent-facing read primitive**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/.mcp.json`

Because all data is in IndexedDB (browser-only, no server-side storage), an agent cannot read the current value of `tapForgivenessThreshold` without loading the page in a browser. This is an architectural characteristic of the entire app, not specific to this PR, but it means context parity (an agent seeing what the user sees) requires a running browser session. This is acceptable given the local-first design but should be noted when planning any future agent workflow that needs to inspect or report settings state.

---

### What's Working Well

- The `useSettings().update()` function is a clean composable primitive: it accepts a `Partial<SettingsDoc>` patch and handles both insert and upsert. There is no business logic encoded in the tool path -- the slider calls `update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: v })` directly with a numeric value, not a string enum or a decision.
- The RxDB schema enforces `minimum: 8, maximum: 60` on `tapForgivenessThreshold` as a hard constraint, so any agent that does call the update path cannot write an out-of-range value.
- `useDraggableTile.ts` reads `settings.tapForgivenessThreshold` from `useSettings()` and passes it straight through to `useTouchDrag` -- no intermediate translation layer that could diverge.
- The existing test coverage in `useTouchDrag.test.ts` and `useDraggableTile.test.tsx` uses the threshold as a concrete value (20) rather than mocking the concept away, which means the feature is testable without a browser.

---

### Score

- **1/4 high-priority capabilities are reliably agent-accessible** (the schema primitive write path exists but requires a Playwright slider interaction; there is no direct programmatic path)
- **Verdict: NEEDS WORK** -- the tap forgiveness feature extends an existing settings parity gap. No new gap category is introduced, but the gap grows with each new settings field that lacks a direct agent-callable write path. The immediate fix (adding `tapForgivenessThreshold` to the Storybook demo) is a one-line change. The structural fix (a patch script or browser-evaluate wrapper) should be tracked to address the entire settings family at once.

Without this, any user who already has a version-0 settings document stored in IndexedDB will encounter a schema fingerprint mismatch when RxDB initialises, causing the collection to be unusable or throwing a hard error on startup."

      "suggested_fix": "Inspect the return value of placeInNextSlot the same way handleClick does. When result.rejected is true, dispatch REJECT_TAP, call playSound('wrong', 0.8) (unless skin.suppressDefaultSounds), and call flashBankTileRejectFeedback. The tap-forgiveness code path should give the same rejection feedback as a direct tap; omitting it means the child hears nothing and sees no shake when they drag-miss a wrong tile at a close distance."
    },
    {
      "title": "tapForgivenessThreshold and onTapFallback are independently optional but always required together — TypeScript does not enforce the pairing",
      "severity": "P2",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts",
      "line": 72,
      "confidence": 75,
      "autofix_class": "types",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Replace the two independent optional fields with a discriminated union on UseTouchDragOptions: `| { tapForgivenessThreshold?: undefined; onTapFallback?: undefined } | { tapForgivenessThreshold: number; onTapFallback: () => void }`. This makes the compiler reject callers that pass one without the other, and removes the need for the runtime double-check `tapForgivenessThreshold !== undefined && onTapFallbackRef.current` — the presence of either guarantees the other."
    },
    {
      "title": "Ghost element removed manually before cleanup() in tap forgiveness path, bypassing the re-entrancy guard",
      "severity": "P3",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts",

      "confidence": 50,
      "autofix_class": "refactor",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": true,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Remove the manual ghost teardown on lines 308-309 and rely solely on cleanup() → cleanupGhost(). cleanupGhost already handles the re-entrancy guard via cleanupInProgressRef, removes the ghost, and releases pointer capture atomically. Doing a partial teardown before calling cleanup creates a window where the lostpointercapture handler could fire and call onDragCancelRef.current() despite the intent being a tap. The only reason to remove the ghost early would be to prevent it from interfering with the subsequent elementsFromPoint call — but cleanup() is called after the decision is already made, so there is no interference risk if the ghost is left for cleanup() to handle."
    },

Write `const tapForgiveness = settings.tapForgivenessThreshold;` to make the type guarantee visible and consistent with how volume and speechRate are handled one day when those also lose their fallbacks."

<summary>Agent "Agent-native parity review" completed</summary>
<result>I now have everything I need to produce the full review. Let me compile the findings.

---

## Agent-Native Architecture Review

### Summary

BaseSkill is a local-first educational game app (React + TanStack Router + RxDB/IndexedDB). Agent integration is provided entirely through the Playwright MCP server configured in `.mcp.json` -- there are no in-process tool definitions (no `streamText`, no `tool()` registrations, no OpenAI/Anthropic SDK tool calls in the source tree). The Playwright MCP means an agent can drive the browser UI but cannot operate on the data layer directly. The tap forgiveness PR adds one new user-facing setting (`tapForgivenessThreshold`) accessible only through the Settings screen slider. The review below evaluates whether an agent retains parity with that new capability.

---

### Capability Map

| UI Action | Location | Agent Tool | In Prompt? | Priority | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Drag slider to set tap forgiveness threshold (8-60 px) | `src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx:136-145` | Playwright MCP `mouse_move` + `mouse_click` (browser automation) | No | Must Have | Partial -- see Finding 1 |
| Read current tapForgivenessThreshold value | `useSettings()` hook / RxDB `settings` doc | No direct DB read; requires navigating to settings page via Playwright | No | Must Have | Gap -- see Finding 2 |
| Programmatically patch any settings field (volume, speechRate, ttsEnabled, showSubtitles, preferredVoiceURI) | `useSettings().update()` | None -- same gap applies to all pre-existing settings fields | No | Must Have | Pre-existing gap |

---

### Findings

#### Critical (Must Fix)

None that are newly introduced by this PR. The structural gap (no programmatic settings write path for agents) pre-dates this PR and is described as a Warning below.

#### Warnings (Should Fix)

**1. No programmatic write path for tapForgivenessThreshold -- agent must drive a slider via Playwright**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx:136-145` and `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.ts:59-75`

The only agent-accessible path to set `tapForgivenessThreshold` is to navigate the Playwright MCP to `/$locale/settings`, locate the slider, and perform pointer interactions. This is fragile: the slider uses `onValueChange` fired by pointer events, so keyboard or direct value injection via `input` field attributes will not trigger the RxDB write. The `useSettings().update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: v })` function is a clean primitive that already accepts a numeric patch -- it is not exposed in any server-side action, CLI script, or agent-callable endpoint.

This gap is not new (all other settings fields have the same problem), but this PR extends the affected surface by one field without improving parity.

Recommendation: Expose a thin Node.js script (similar to the existing `scripts/dev/dump-db.js` pattern visible in the root git status) or a dedicated RxDB seed/patch utility that accepts JSON patches and writes them directly to the IndexedDB storage file. Alternatively, add an `update-settings` Playwright MCP helper that takes a key-value argument and calls the hook programmatically via browser `evaluate`, removing the fragile slider interaction requirement.

**2. tapForgivenessThreshold is absent from the useSettings demo and any agent-visible surface that describes available settings fields**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.demo.tsx:10-16`

The `UseSettingsDemo` component that is rendered in Storybook lists `volume`, `speechRate`, `ttsEnabled`, and `showSubtitles` but does not render `tapForgivenessThreshold`. An agent using Storybook MCP to understand the settings schema would miss the new field entirely. The demo is the de facto living documentation for what settings exist.

Recommendation: Add a `tapForgivenessThreshold` display line to `useSettings.demo.tsx` to match the other fields.

#### Observations

**3. DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX (8 px, hardcoded) and tapForgivenessThreshold serve different purposes but the relationship is not documented**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:4` and `:197-200`

`DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX` is the minimum displacement to initiate a drag; `tapForgivenessThreshold` is the maximum displacement at pointer-up to retroactively classify a completed drag as a tap. Both are in pixel units, the schema minimum for `tapForgivenessThreshold` is 8 (matching `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX`), but that constraint relationship is implicit. An agent asked to reason about tuning touch sensitivity has no context to know that setting `tapForgivenessThreshold` below `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX` would create a dead zone where a gesture is neither a tap nor a recognised drag. A code comment co-located with the schema constraint, or a validation note in the settings hook, would give an agent enough context to avoid recommending an invalid configuration.

**4. The useSettings unit test suite does not include a test for tapForgivenessThreshold default or persistence**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/hooks/useSettings.test.tsx`

The test file covers `volume` and `speechRate` but not the new field. The `useDraggableTile` and `useTouchDrag` test files do exercise the threshold at the integration level, but a unit test confirming that `useSettings` returns `20` as the default for `tapForgivenessThreshold` and that `update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: 40 })` persists correctly would complete coverage consistent with the existing test pattern and satisfy the TDD requirement stated in CLAUDE.md.

**5. Playwright MCP is the only registered MCP server in `.mcp.json`; the settings collection has no agent-facing read primitive**

`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/.mcp.json`

Because all data is in IndexedDB (browser-only, no server-side storage), an agent cannot read the current value of `tapForgivenessThreshold` without loading the page in a browser. This is an architectural characteristic of the entire app, not specific to this PR, but it means context parity (an agent seeing what the user sees) requires a running browser session. This is acceptable given the local-first design but should be noted when planning any future agent workflow that needs to inspect or report settings state.

---

### What's Working Well

- The `useSettings().update()` function is a clean composable primitive: it accepts a `Partial&lt;SettingsDoc&gt;` patch and handles both insert and upsert. There is no business logic encoded in the tool path -- the slider calls `update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: v })` directly with a numeric value, not a string enum or a decision.
- The RxDB schema enforces `minimum: 8, maximum: 60` on `tapForgivenessThreshold` as a hard constraint, so any agent that does call the update path cannot write an out-of-range value.
- `useDraggableTile.ts` reads `settings.tapForgivenessThreshold` from `useSettings()` and passes it straight through to `useTouchDrag` -- no intermediate translation layer that could diverge.
- The existing test coverage in `useTouchDrag.test.ts` and `useDraggableTile.test.tsx` uses the threshold as a concrete value (20) rather than mocking the concept away, which means the feature is testable without a browser.

---

### Score

- **1/4 high-priority capabilities are reliably agent-accessible** (the schema primitive write path exists but requires a Playwright slider interaction; there is no direct programmatic path)
- **Verdict: NEEDS WORK** -- the tap forgiveness feature extends an existing settings parity gap. No new gap category is introduced, but the gap grows with each new settings field that lacks a direct agent-callable write path. The immediate fix (adding `tapForgivenessThreshold` to the Storybook demo) is a one-line change. The structural fix (a patch script or browser-evaluate wrapper) should be tracked to address the entire settings family at once.</result>

</task-notification>

Commit e4eb2743 added useTouchDrag.test.ts (199 new lines) and the tap-forgiveness logic in useTouchDrag.ts (54 new lines) together in a single commit. CLAUDE.md § Test-Driven Development – Bug fixes: 'Write a failing test that reproduces the bug before touching production code.' The fix should have been split: one commit adding the failing test, a second commit adding the implementation that makes it pass."
    }

  "residual_risks": [

      "title": "No unit tests for the settings UI slider (U4)",
      "description": "The plan (U4 Test scenarios) called for a test that the slider renders with the current setting value and that sliding calls update({ tapForgivenessThreshold: v }). The gap means a regression in the settings page UI would be undetected by automated checks.",
      "severity": "P3"
    },
    {
      "title": "Docs committed after code — docs commit ordering is unusual but conformant",
      "description": "The implementation commits (e4eb2743, f5031e5f, dcb8b17c) landed before the docs commit (1077c7a1) and the brainstorm/plan files (fea0c5c6). CLAUDE.md states 'Every task starts with git worktree add ... this includes writing docs, specs, and plans' and 'Planning files committed directly to master bypass the PR review process.' Because all commits are on the branch and none are on master, the worktree rule is satisfied. However committing plans after implementation reverses the stated intent ('Plans are binding source of truth for executor agents'). This is a process smell rather than a hard rule violation, but reviewers should note the plan was written post-implementation.",
      "severity": "P3"
    }
  ],
  "testing_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "Settings slider in settings.tsx has no automated test",
      "description": "Plan unit U4 listed three test scenarios for the tap forgiveness slider (renders with default, onValueChange calls update, no settings doc shows default 20). None were implemented. The settings.tsx file has no corresponding test file in the changed file list.",
      "file": "src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx"
    }
  ]
}
ENDJSON

```json
{
  "reviewer": "kieran-typescript",
  "findings": [
    {
      "title": "RxDB settings schema version not bumped after adding tapForgivenessThreshold",

      "line": 19,

      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Bump version to 1 and add a passthrough migration in create-database.ts: `settings: { schema: settingsSchema, migrationStrategies: { 1: (doc) => doc } }`. The new field is optional with a default, so the identity migration is correct. Without this, any user who already has a version-0 settings document stored in IndexedDB will encounter a schema fingerprint mismatch when RxDB initialises, causing the collection to be unusable or throwing a hard error on startup."
    },
    {
      "title": "onTapFallback silently drops rejected placements — no audio or visual feedback",
      "severity": "P2",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useDraggableTile.ts",
      "line": 144,
      "confidence": 75,
      "autofix_class": "logic",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": true,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Inspect the return value of placeInNextSlot the same way handleClick does. When result.rejected is true, dispatch REJECT_TAP, call playSound('wrong', 0.8) (unless skin.suppressDefaultSounds), and call flashBankTileRejectFeedback. The tap-forgiveness code path should give the same rejection feedback as a direct tap; omitting it means the child hears nothing and sees no shake when they drag-miss a wrong tile at a close distance."
    },
    {
      "title": "tapForgivenessThreshold and onTapFallback are independently optional but always required together — TypeScript does not enforce the pairing",
      "severity": "P2",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts",
      "line": 72,
      "confidence": 75,
      "autofix_class": "types",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Replace the two independent optional fields with a discriminated union on UseTouchDragOptions: `| { tapForgivenessThreshold?: undefined; onTapFallback?: undefined } | { tapForgivenessThreshold: number; onTapFallback: () => void }`. This makes the compiler reject callers that pass one without the other, and removes the need for the runtime double-check `tapForgivenessThreshold !== undefined && onTapFallbackRef.current` — the presence of either guarantees the other."
    },
    {
      "title": "Ghost element removed manually before cleanup() in tap forgiveness path, bypassing the re-entrancy guard",
      "severity": "P3",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts",
      "line": 308,
      "confidence": 50,
      "autofix_class": "refactor",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": true,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "suggested_fix": "Remove the manual ghost teardown on lines 308-309 and rely solely on cleanup() → cleanupGhost(). cleanupGhost already handles the re-entrancy guard via cleanupInProgressRef, removes the ghost, and releases pointer capture atomically. Doing a partial teardown before calling cleanup creates a window where the lostpointercapture handler could fire and call onDragCancelRef.current() despite the intent being a tap."
    },
    {
      "title": "tapForgiveness ?? 20 fallback in settings.tsx is redundant — useSettings guarantees the field is always a number",
      "severity": "P3",
      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx",

      "autofix_class": "cleanup",
      "owner": "author",
      "requires_verification": false,
      "pre_existing": false,
      "suggested_fix": "The return type of useSettings is `typeof DEFAULT_SETTINGS & Partial<SettingsDoc>`. Because DEFAULT_SETTINGS includes `tapForgivenessThreshold: 20`, the intersection resolves the field to `number` (non-optional). The `?? 20` is unreachable dead code. Write `const tapForgiveness = settings.tapForgivenessThreshold;` directly."
    }
  ],

This is inconsistent with the drop detection priority order.",

      "file": "/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/components/answer-game/useDraggableTile.test.tsx",

      "confidence": 50
    },

Findings written to `/tmp/compound-engineering/ce-code-review/20260502-132625-7ce50660/kieran-typescript.json`.

**Summary of what I found and why:**

**P1 — Schema version not bumped** (`/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/src/db/schemas/settings.ts` line 19, confidence 100). The `settingsSchema` stays at `version: 0` despite adding a new property. The project has `RxDBMigrationSchemaPlugin` installed and other schemas (bookmarks, custom_games, saved_game_configs, session_history_index) all carry a version bump plus a `migrationStrategies` entry in `create-database.ts` whenever they gain fields. The settings collection at line 67 of `create-database.ts` has no migration strategies and will break for any existing user when the schema fingerprint changes.

**P2 — Silent rejection on tap fallback** (`useDraggableTile.ts` line 144, confidence 75). `onTapFallback` calls `placeInNextSlot(tile.id)` and discards the result. The `handleClick` path on the same tile checks `result.rejected` and branches into `REJECT_TAP` dispatch, `playSound('wrong', 0.8)`, and `flashBankTileRejectFeedback`. A child who micro-drags a wrong tile will hear and see nothing on rejection, breaking the feedback contract that the rest of the interaction model relies on.

**P2 — Unpaired optional parameters** (`useTouchDrag.ts` line 72, confidence 75). `tapForgivenessThreshold?: number` and `onTapFallback?: () => void` are independently optional, but the feature only activates when both are set. TypeScript does not enforce the pairing. The runtime double-check `tapForgivenessThreshold !== undefined && onTapFallbackRef.current` is the only guard. A discriminated union removes the ambiguity without adding ceremony.

**P3 — Manual ghost removal before cleanup()** (`useTouchDrag.ts` line 308, confidence 50). The tap path manually zeroes `ghostRef` before calling `cleanup()`, which calls `cleanupGhost()`. This creates a gap outside `cleanupInProgressRef` where a synchronous `lostpointercapture` event could still invoke `onDragCancelRef.current()`. The existing `cleanupGhost` machinery is already designed to handle teardown safely; the manual teardown is inconsistent with it.

**P3 — Redundant `?? 20` in settings.tsx** (line 57, confidence 50). `useSettings` returns `typeof DEFAULT_SETTINGS & Partial<SettingsDoc>` and `DEFAULT_SETTINGS` includes `tapForgivenessThreshold: 20`, so the type resolves to `number`. The fallback is dead code that hides the type guarantee.

---

Because all commits are on the branch and none are on master, the worktree rule is satisfied. However committing plans after implementation reverses the stated intent ('Plans are binding source of truth for executor agents'). This is a process smell rather than a hard rule violation, but reviewers should note the plan was written post-implementation.",
      "severity": "P3"
    }
  ],
  "testing_gaps": [
    {
      "title": "Settings slider in settings.tsx has no automated test",
      "description": "Plan unit U4 listed three test scenarios for the tap forgiveness slider (renders with default, onValueChange calls update, no settings doc shows default 20). None were implemented. The settings.tsx file has no corresponding test file in the changed file list.",
      "file": "src/routes/$locale/_app/settings.tsx"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Summary of what was checked and why each item was accepted or flagged:**

**Architecture docs (CLAUDE.md § Architecture Documentation):** The rule requires updating co-located `.mdx` docs "in the same PR" when modifying `src/components/answer-game/` files. Commit `1077c7a1` added the tap forgiveness mermaid diagram to `AnswerGame.flows.mdx`. This is compliant — same PR, different commit is acceptable.

**React component style (CLAUDE.md § React Best Practices):** `SettingsScreen` in `settings.tsx` is defined as `const SettingsScreen = () => ...`, which is correct. The `function` declarations in `useSettings.ts` (`useSettings`, `plainSettingsFromQuery`) are hooks/utilities, not React components, so the `react/function-component-definition` ESLint rule does not apply to them. Both function declarations were also pre-existing on master — this PR only added one line to `DEFAULT_SETTINGS`.

**Named exports:** No `export default` usage was introduced. `useTouchDrag`, `useDraggableTile`, and `useSettings` all use named exports. `SettingsScreen` is not directly exported (only `Route` is). Compliant.

**Worktree workflow:** All commits are on branch `298-tap-forgiveness` under `worktrees/298-tap-forgiveness/`. No direct master commits. Compliant.

**Markdown linting:** Both new `.md` files pass `markdownlint-cli2` and `npx prettier --check` with zero errors.

**TDD (P2 finding):** The single violation found is that `useTouchDrag.test.ts` (the new test file) was created in the same commit (`e4eb2743`) as the production logic in `useTouchDrag.ts`. CLAUDE.md § Test-Driven Development – Bug fixes rule 1 explicitly states "Write a failing test that reproduces the bug before touching production code." A separate prior commit containing only the failing test is required.

---

## Institutional Learnings Search Results

### Search Context

- **Feature/Task**: Adding "tap forgiveness" to `useTouchDrag` — micro-drags below a displacement threshold are treated as taps instead of cancelled drags. Threshold should be configurable via settings persistence.
- **Keywords Used**: `useTouchDrag`, `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX`, `tap`, `micro-drag`, `drag forgiveness`, `pointer events`, `gesture recognition`, `bank drop`, `onDropOnBank`, `onDragCancel`, `settings schema`, `SettingsDoc`
- **Files Scanned**: All non-node_modules `.md`/`.mdx` files under `docs/`, `src/`, `.specstory/history/` — no `docs/solutions/` directory exists in this repo
- **Relevant Matches**: 5

---

### Critical Patterns

No `docs/solutions/patterns/critical-patterns.md` exists in this repo.

---

### Relevant Learnings

#### 1. Issue #298 Root Cause Analysis — Micro-Drag Treated as Failed Drop

- **File**: `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/.specstory/history/2026-05-01_13-26-46Z-we-tried-to-fix.md`
- **Module**: `answer-game / useTouchDrag`
- **Problem Type**: `runtime_error` (inferred — user-visible interaction failure)
- **Relevance**: This is the direct predecessor session for this PR. The exact problem, design decision, and user preference were all captured here.
- **Key Insight**: The root cause is that `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX = 8` causes a finger that drifts 8+ px and releases back over the bank to hit `onDropOnBank`, which does nothing — the tile speaks but is never placed. PR #287 (fast-tap queue) solved a *different* problem (rapid sequential taps arriving before state updates); it did not address micro-drags. The agreed design: **if total displacement from start is under a configurable forgiveness threshold AND the pointer lifts over the bank area, treat it as a tap (fire `useAutoNextSlot` placement)**. Time-based detection was considered but rejected as overkill — displacement alone is sufficient. The threshold must be exposed in the game settings UI so it can be tuned against real-user data.

#### 2. Frozen Ghost Bug — Ghost Lifecycle Must Be Defended at Every Terminal Event

- **File**: `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-07-unified-slot-component-design.md`
- **Module**: `answer-game / useTouchDrag` (Section 5: Mobile Drag Bug Fix)
- **Problem Type**: `runtime_error` (inferred — frozen ghost overlay on mobile)
- **Relevance**: The current `useTouchDrag.ts` already implements the three-part fix described here, but the fix history is important context: a ghost that doesn't clean up can survive indefinitely. The tap-forgiveness path is a new terminal event — it must call `cleanup()` reliably, exactly as the existing drop paths do.
- **Key Insight**: Every code path that resolves a pointer gesture — drop, cancel, bank drop, bank-tile drop, AND now tap-forgiveness — must call `cleanup()`. The 5-second safety timer (`safetyTimerRef`) and `lostpointercapture` handler exist as belt-and-suspenders, but the primary cleanup must be synchronous on the tap-forgiveness branch. Missing a `cleanup()` call on the new path would recreate the frozen ghost bug.

#### 3. `onPointerUp` Priority Order — Tap Forgiveness Must Not Break Existing Drop Priorities

- **File**: `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/docs/spec-swap-slot-bank.md`
- **Module**: `answer-game / useTouchDrag`
- **Problem Type**: `architecture_pattern` (inferred)
- **Relevance**: The current `onPointerUp` has a documented 7-step priority ladder: center→zone, center→bank-tile, center→bank, corners→zone, corners→bank-tile, corners→bank, cancel. Tap forgiveness needs to insert cleanly into this priority scheme without accidentally triggering on real drags that happen to end over the bank.
- **Key Insight**: The forgiveness check belongs as a **guard at step 7 (cancel)** — only when all six drop-target checks have failed AND the total displacement is under the threshold should it fire as a tap. Placing it earlier (e.g., at center→bank) would swallow intentional drag-then-return-to-bank gestures. The condition is: `!isDragging.current` (never crossed threshold) OR (`isDragging.current` AND displacement `< forgivenessThreshold` AND the ghost end-point is over the bank or nowhere specific).

#### 4. `SettingsDoc` Has No Drag/Tap Fields — Adding One Requires a Schema Migration

- **File**: `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/src/db/schemas/settings.ts`
- **Module**: `db / settings`
- **Problem Type**: `convention` (inferred — RxDB schema versioning)
- **Relevance**: The user explicitly requested the forgiveness threshold be exposed in settings for tuning. The current `SettingsDoc` (version `0`) has no drag-related field. RxDB schemas require a version bump and migration when fields are added.
- **Key Insight**: Adding `tapForgivenessThreshold?: number` to `SettingsDoc` requires bumping `settingsSchema.version` from `0` to `1` and providing a migration strategy. Without the version bump, existing documents will fail RxDB validation on upgrade. Check `src/db/hooks/useSettings.ts` for how other optional numeric fields (e.g., `volume`, `speechRate`) are consumed and what default-fallback pattern is used there — new numeric settings follow the same pattern.

#### 5. Drag Preview `onHoverZone` — Callback Refs Pattern for Stable Identities

- **File**: `/Users/leocaseiro/Sites/base-skill/docs/superpowers/specs/2026-04-08-drag-preview-sound-fix-design.md`
- **Module**: `answer-game / useTouchDrag`
- **Problem Type**: `design_pattern` (inferred)
- **Relevance**: The existing `useTouchDrag` stores all caller callbacks in refs (`onDragCancelRef`, `onHoverZoneRef`, `onHoverBankTileRef`) to avoid stale closures inside pointer-event handlers. Any new callback added for tap-forgiveness (e.g., a caller-supplied `onTap`) must follow this same ref pattern.
- **Key Insight**: New callbacks passed to `useTouchDrag` must be stored in a `useRef` + `useEffect` sync pattern (see lines 84–97 of the current source). Passing a callback directly into `useCallback` deps instead of using a ref will cause stale-closure bugs where the tap handler fires with an outdated closure over state. The existing `onDragCancelRef` is the canonical example to mirror.

---

### Recommendations

- **Forgiveness threshold placement in `onPointerUp`**: Insert the micro-drag-as-tap check at the very end of the priority ladder (after step 6, before the cancel path), not at step 3 (center→bank). This preserves intentional drag-cancel-by-returning-to-bank.
- **Displacement measurement**: Measure Euclidean distance (`Math.sqrt(dx*dx + dy*dy)`) from `startPos.current` to the `pointerUp` coordinate. Compare against a `tapForgivenessThreshold` drawn from settings (with a sensible default, e.g. 20–30 px, matching the brainstormed 20–30 px range in the issue session).
- **Guard against `isDragging.current = false` edge**: When the pointer is released before the 8 px drag threshold is crossed, `isDragging.current` is `false` and the existing code falls straight through to `cleanup()` without calling any drop handler — that is the existing tap path already handled by `useDraggableTile.handleClick`. The forgiveness logic is only needed when `isDragging.current === true` (a ghost was created) and the drop lands nowhere productive.
- **`cleanup()` discipline**: Whichever branch fires the tap-forgiveness placement must call `cleanup()` synchronously. Use the same `onDrop`-path cleanup pattern, not a deferred one.
- **Settings schema migration**: Bump `settingsSchema.version` to `1` and add a migration entry when adding `tapForgivenessThreshold`. Check `useSettings.ts` for the `volume`/`speechRate` fallback pattern to replicate for a numeric optional field.
- **Callback ref for any new `onTap` prop**: Store it as a ref (mirror `onDragCancelRef`) rather than closing over it in `useCallback` deps to avoid stale closures.
- **PR #287 does not conflict**: The fast-tap queue in `useAutoNextSlot` (pending placements + bank pre-validation) already handles the placement side. The tap-forgiveness path in `useTouchDrag` only needs to call the same `onTap`/placement callback that a direct tap would — it rides the existing queue mechanism naturally.

---

---

## Code Review: Tap Forgiveness (#298)

**Scope:** `298-tap-forgiveness` vs `origin/master` (12 files, ~739 insertions)
**Intent:** Add distance-based tap forgiveness to `useTouchDrag` — micro-drags below a configurable threshold are treated as taps. Threshold persisted per-profile, adjustable via Settings slider.
**Reviewers:** correctness, testing, maintainability, project-standards, kieran-typescript, agent-native, learnings-researcher

---

### P1 — Critical

| # | File | Issue | Reviewer | Conf | Route |
|---|------|-------|----------|------|-------|
| 1 | `settings.ts:19` | RxDB schema version not bumped after adding `tapForgivenessThreshold` — existing users with a stored version-0 settings doc will hit a schema fingerprint mismatch on startup | kieran-typescript (confirmed by learnings-researcher) | 100 | manual → downstream-resolver |

### P2 — High

| # | File | Issue | Reviewer | Conf | Route |
|---|------|-------|----------|------|-------|
| 2 | `useDraggableTile.ts:144` | `onTapFallback` discards `placeInNextSlot` result — no shake, sound, REJECT_TAP dispatch, or game:evaluate event when a wrong tile is placed via micro-drag forgiveness (unlike `handleClick`) | correctness, maintainability, kieran-typescript | 100 | manual → downstream-resolver, needs verification |
| 3 | `useTouchDrag.ts:72` | `tapForgivenessThreshold` and `onTapFallback` are independently optional but only meaningful together — TypeScript doesn't enforce the pairing | kieran-typescript | 75 | manual → downstream-resolver |

### P3 — Low

| # | File | Issue | Reviewer | Conf | Route |
|---|------|-------|----------|------|-------|
| 4 | `useTouchDrag.ts:308` | Ghost element manually removed before `cleanup()` — duplicates teardown and creates brief window outside `cleanupInProgressRef` guard | correctness (P3), maintainability (P2), kieran-typescript (P3) — kept P3: correctness notes it's "harmless" | 75 | advisory |
| 5 | `useTouchDrag.test.ts` | TDD: test and production code committed together, not test-first per CLAUDE.md | project-standards | 75 | advisory |

---

### Learnings & Past Solutions

The learnings-researcher confirmed:
- **Schema migration required** — project convention bumps version + adds migration strategy for every field addition (matching finding #1)
- **Callback ref pattern correctly followed** — `onTapFallbackRef` mirrors the existing `onDragCancelRef` pattern
- **`cleanup()` is called on the tap path** — satisfies the frozen-ghost-bug discipline
- **Forgiveness check placement is correct** — fires inside `isDragging.current` guard, after drag started but before normal drop detection

### Agent-Native Gaps

No new gap category introduced. The tap forgiveness threshold extends the pre-existing pattern where all settings fields (volume, speechRate, etc.) lack a direct programmatic write path — agents must drive the browser UI via Playwright MCP. The `useSettings().update()` primitive is clean but not agent-callable without a browser session.

### Coverage

- **Suppressed:** 1 finding below confidence 75 (redundant `?? 20` fallback in settings.tsx)
- **Testing gaps (from testing reviewer):** no test for `isDragging=false` micro-tap path; no test for `threshold=undefined` + callback provided; no negative assertion on `speakTile` in onTapFallback test; no `useSettings` default test for new field
- **Residual risk:** tap forgiveness is unreachable for ultra-fast taps that never cross `DRAG_THRESHOLD_PX=8` (these fire the normal click handler instead, which is correct behavior)
- **Failed reviewers:** none

---

### Verdict: **Not ready**

Finding #1 (schema version) is a P1 that will break existing users on upgrade. Finding #2 (missing reject feedback) creates an inconsistent UX where micro-drag placements of wrong tiles give no feedback. Fix order: #1 → #2 → optionally #3.

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-02 04:27:21Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="Read"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Read**</summary>
 `/Users/leocaseiro/.claude/plugins/cache/compound-engineering-plugin/compound-engineering/3.3.2/skills/ce-code-review/references/walkthrough.md`

```text
1	# Per-finding Walk-through
2	
3	This reference defines Interactive mode's per-finding walk-through — the path the user enters by picking option A (`Review each finding one by one — accept the recommendation or choose another action`) from the routing question. It also covers the unified completion report that every terminal path (walk-through, best-judgment, File tickets, zero findings) emits.
4	
5	Interactive mode only.
6	
7	---
8	
9	## Entry
10	
11	The walk-through receives, from the orchestrator:
12	
13	- The merged findings list in severity order (P0 → P1 → P2 → P3), filtered to `gated_auto` and `manual` findings that survived the Stage 5 anchor gate (anchor 75+, with P0 escape at anchor 50). Advisory findings are included when they were surfaced to this phase (advisory findings normally live in the report-only queue, but when the review flow routes them here for acknowledgment they take the advisory variant below).
14	- The cached tracker-detection tuple from `tracker-defer.md` (`{ tracker_name, confidence, named_sink_available, any_sink_available }`). `any_sink_available` determines whether the Defer option is offered; `named_sink_available` + `confidence` determine whether the label names the tracker inline.
15	- The run id for artifact lookups.
16	
17	Each finding's recommended action has already been normalized by Stage 5 (step 7b — tie-break on action). The walk-through surfaces that recommendation to the user but does not recompute it.
18	
19	---
20	
21	## Per-finding presentation
22	
23	Each finding is presented in two parts: a **terminal output block** carrying the explanation, and a **question** via the platform's blocking question tool (`AskUserQuestion` in Claude Code, `request_user_input` in Codex, `ask_user` in Gemini, `ask_user` in Pi (requires the `pi-ask-user` extension)) carrying the decision. Never merge the two — the terminal block uses markdown; the question uses plain text.
24	
25	In Claude Code the tool should already be loaded from the Interactive-mode pre-load step in `SKILL.md` — if it isn't, call `ToolSearch` with query `select:AskUserQuestion` now. Fall back to presenting the per-finding options as a numbered list only when the harness genuinely lacks a blocking tool — `ToolSearch` returns no match, the tool call explicitly fails, or the runtime mode does not expose it (e.g., Codex edit modes without `request_user_input`). A pending schema load is not a fallback trigger. Never silently skip the question.
26	
27	### Terminal output block (print before firing the question)
28	
29	Render as markdown. Labels on their own line, blank lines between sections:
30	
31	```
32	## Finding {N} of {M} — {severity} {plain-English title}
33	
34	{file}:{line}
35	
36	**What's wrong**
37	
38	{plain-English problem statement from why_it_matters}
39	
40	**Proposed fix**
41	
42	{suggested_fix — rendered per the substitution rules below: prose-first, intent-language}
43	
44	**Why it works**
45	
46	{short reasoning, grounded in a codebase pattern when available}
47	
48	{R15 conflict context line, when applicable}
49	```
50	
51	Substitutions:
52	
53	- **`{plain-English title}`:** a 3-8 word summary suitable as a heading. Derived from the merged finding's `title` field but rephrased so it reads as observable behavior (e.g., "Path traversal in loadUserFromCache" rather than "Missing userId validation on line 36").
54	- **`why_it_matters`:** read the contributing reviewer's artifact file at `/tmp/compound-engineering/ce-code-review/{run_id}/{reviewer_name}.json` using the same `file + line_bucket(line, +/-3) + normalize(title)` matching that headless mode uses (see `SKILL.md` Stage 6 detail enrichment). When multiple reviewers flagged the merged finding, try them in the order they appear in the merged finding's reviewer list. Use the first match.
55	- **`suggested_fix`:** from the merged finding's `suggested_fix` field. Render as prose describing **intent**, not as syntax. The fixer subagent owns the exact code — the walk-through just needs enough for the user to trust or reject the action. Rules:
56	  - **Default — one sentence describing the effect.** What does the fix achieve, and where does it live? Prefer intent language over quoted code.
57	    - ✅ `Throw on non-2xx response before parsing JSON.`
58	    - ✅ `` Replace `==` with `===` on line 42. ``
59	    - ✅ `` Add a `response.ok` check after the fetch and throw on non-2xx. ``
60	    - ✅ `Extract the request-building logic into a helper and call it from both sites.`
61	    - ❌ `` Add `if (!response.ok) throw new Error(`HTTP ${response.status}`);` after the `await fetch(...)` call, before `response.json()`. `` — nested backticks, multiple code spans, full statement quoted; renders broken in terminal.
62	  - **Code-span budget: at most 2 inline backtick spans per sentence, each a single identifier, operator, or short phrase** (e.g., `` `response.ok` ``, `` `===` ``, `` `fetchUserById` ``). Never embed full statements, template literals, or code requiring nested backticks. If the intent can't be stated within that budget, the prose is too close to syntax — restate at a higher level, or switch to summary + artifact pointer.
63	  - **Always leave a space before and after every backtick span.** Without it, the terminal's markdown renderer eats the delimiters and runs the words together.
64	  - **Raw code block — only for short (≤5 line) genuinely additive new code** where no before-state exists (new file, new function, new guard at the top of an empty body). Above 5 lines, switch to summary + pointer.
65	  - **Summary + artifact pointer** — when prose can't capture the fix: one-sentence transformation + key symbol/location + `Full fix: /tmp/compound-engineering/ce-code-review/{run_id}/{reviewer_name}.json → findings[].suggested_fix`.
66	  - **No diff blocks.** Modifications to existing code render as prose.
67	- **`Why it works`:** grounded reasoning that, where possible, references a similar pattern already used elsewhere in the codebase (e.g., "matches the format-validation pattern already used at src/cli/io.ts:41"). One to three sentences.
68	- **R15 conflict context line (when applicable):** when contributing reviewers implied different actions for this finding and Stage 5 step 7b broke the tie, surface that briefly. Example: `Correctness recommends Apply; Testing recommends Skip (low confidence). Agent's recommendation: Skip.` The orchestrator's recommendation — the post-tie-break value — is what the menu labels "recommended."
69	
70	When no artifact match exists for the finding (merge-synthesized finding, or the persona's artifact write failed), the terminal block degrades to the heading + `suggested_fix` only (omit the `What's wrong` and `Why it works` sections) and records the gap for the Coverage section of the completion report.
71	
72	### Question stem (short, decision-focused)
73	
74	After the terminal block renders, fire the platform's blocking question tool with a compact two-line stem:
75	
76	```
77	Finding {N} of {M} — {severity} {short handle}.
78	{Action framing in a phrase}?
79	```
80	
81	Where:
82	
83	- **Short handle:** matches the `{plain-English title}` from the terminal block heading.
84	- **Action framing:** one phrase describing what the *single recommended action* does, as a yes/no question. Examples: `Apply the format-validation + path.resolve guard?`, `Skip the fix since the fixture is being deleted?`, `Defer and file a rotation ticket?`.
85	
86	Never enumerate alternatives in the stem. One recommendation as a yes/no — the option list carries the alternatives. When the recommendation is close, surface the disagreement in the R15 conflict context line, not as a multi-option stem.
87	
88	Example (recommendation = Apply):
89	
90	```
91	Finding 3 of 8 — P1 path traversal in loadUserFromCache.
92	Apply the format-validation + path.resolve guard?
93	```
94	
95	Example (recommendation = Skip because content context overrides default):
96	
97	```
98	Finding 1 of 9 — P0 hardcoded admin token.
99	Skip the fix since the fixture is being deleted?
100	(Security recommends Apply; file context recommends Skip. Agent's recommendation: Skip.)
101	```
102	
103	Never embed code blocks, diff syntax, or the full fix/reasoning in the stem.
104	
105	### Confirmation between findings
106	
107	After the user answers and before printing the next finding's terminal block, emit a one-line confirmation of the action taken. Examples: `→ Applied. Fix staged at src/utils/api-client.ts:36-37.`, `→ Deferred. Ticket filed: <url>.`, `→ Skipped.`, `→ Acknowledged.`
108	
109	### Options (four, or adapted as noted)
110	
111	Fixed order. Never reorder:
112	
113	```
114	1. Apply the proposed fix
115	2. Defer — file a [TRACKER] ticket
116	3. Skip — don't apply, don't track
117	4. Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest
118	```
119	
120	Render the `[TRACKER]` label per `tracker-defer.md`: when `confidence = high` AND `named_sink_available = true`, replace `[TRACKER]` with the concrete tracker name (e.g., `Defer — file a Linear ticket`). When `any_sink_available = true` but either `confidence = low` or `named_sink_available = false`, use the generic whole label `Defer — file a ticket` — whole-label substitution, not a `[TRACKER]` token swap.
121	
122	**Mark the post-tie-break recommendation with `(recommended)` on its option label.** Required, not optional. Any of the four options can carry it:
123	
124	```
125	1. Apply the proposed fix  (recommended)
126	2. Defer — file a ticket
127	3. Skip — don't apply, don't track
128	4. Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest
129	```
130	
131	```
132	1. Apply the proposed fix
133	2. Defer — file a ticket
134	3. Skip — don't apply, don't track  (recommended)
135	4. Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest
136	```
137	
138	When reviewers disagreed or content context cuts against the default, still mark one option — whichever Stage 5 step 7b produced — and surface the disagreement in the R15 conflict context line.
139	
140	### Adaptations
141	
142	- **No `suggested_fix` (Apply suppressed):** when the finding has no concrete `suggested_fix` (`gated_auto` or `manual` with `suggested_fix == null`), option A (`Apply`) is **omitted from the menu**. Stage 5 step 6b already maps these to a `Defer` recommendation, so the `(recommended)` marker lands on a still-visible option. The menu shows three options: `Defer` / `Skip` / `Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest` (and reduces to `Skip` / `Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest` when combined with the no-sink adaptation). When this combines with the advisory variant, the same suppression is moot because option A is already replaced with `Acknowledge`. This rule mirrors the suppression applied during `SKILL.md` Step 2 Interactive option B's post-run `Walk through these one at a time` re-entry, so the same handling applies regardless of which entry path the user came in through.
143	- **Advisory-only finding:** when the finding's `autofix_class` is `advisory` (no actionable fix), option A is replaced with `Acknowledge — mark as reviewed`. The other three options remain. The advisory variant is the only case where `Acknowledge` appears in the menu.
144	- **N=1 (exactly one pending finding):** the terminal block's heading omits `Finding N of M` and renders as `## {severity} {plain-English title}`. The stem's first line drops the position counter, becoming `{severity} {short handle}.` Option D (`Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest`) is suppressed because no subsequent findings exist — the menu shows three options: Apply / Defer / Skip (or Acknowledge, for advisory).
145	- **No sink (Defer option unavailable):** when the tracker-detection tuple reports `any_sink_available: false` (every tier in the fallback chain — named tracker and GitHub Issues via `gh` — is unreachable), option B (`Defer`) is omitted. The stem appends one line explaining that no issue tracker is configured for this checkout (Linear, GitHub Issues, etc., were probed and unavailable). Phrase it for a developer audience — avoid `tracker sink` jargon, and avoid `platform` since the missing piece is per-project, not per-agent-platform. The menu shows three options: Apply / Skip / Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest (and Acknowledge in place of Apply for advisory-only findings). **Before rendering the options, remap any per-finding `Defer` recommendation produced by Stage 5 step 7b to `Skip`** so the `(recommended)` marker always lands on an option that is actually in the menu. When the remap fires, surface it on the R15 conflict context line — name what was downgraded and why (so the reader sees the cross-reviewer Defer recommendation hasn't silently disappeared). This is a render-time runtime step; Stage 5 step 7b has no knowledge of sink availability and only orders conflicting reviewer recommendations.
146	- **Combined N=1 + no sink:** the menu shows two options: Apply / Skip (or Acknowledge / Skip).
147	
148	Only when `ToolSearch` explicitly returns no match or the tool call errors — or on a platform with no blocking question tool — fall back to presenting the options as a numbered list and waiting for the user's next reply.
149	
150	---
151	
152	## Per-finding routing
153	
154	For each finding's answer:
155	
156	- **Apply the proposed fix** — add the finding's id to an in-memory Apply set. Advance to the next finding. Do not dispatch the fixer inline — Apply accumulates for end-of-walk-through batch dispatch.
157	- **Acknowledge — mark as reviewed** (advisory variant) — record Acknowledge in the in-memory decision list. Advance to the next finding. No side effects.
158	- **Defer — file a [TRACKER] ticket** — invoke the tracker-defer flow from `tracker-defer.md`. The walk-through's position indicator stays on the current finding during any failure-path sub-question (Retry / Fall back / Convert to Skip). On success, record the tracker URL / reference in the in-memory decision list and advance. On conversion-to-Skip from the failure path, advance with the failure noted in the completion report.
159	- **Skip — don't apply, don't track** — record Skip in the in-memory decision list. Advance. No side effects.
160	- **Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest** — exit the walk-through loop and dispatch the fixer subagent (`SKILL.md` Step 3) immediately on the remaining action set: the current finding plus everything not yet decided. No Stage 5b pre-pass. No bulk-preview approval gate. The fixer applies items with concrete `suggested_fix`, no-ops on advisory items, and routes items where the fix cannot be applied cleanly (or where evidence no longer matches the code) to a `failed` bucket with a one-line reason. Apply findings the user already picked during the walk-through are dispatched in the same fixer pass — the remaining set joins the in-memory Apply set so the fixer receives the union and applies all changes against a consistent tree. After the fixer returns, follow the post-run failure-handling logic in `SKILL.md` Step 2 Interactive option B — when the `failed` bucket is non-empty, fire one question with three options (file tickets / walk through / ignore). When the `failed` bucket is empty, emit the unified completion report directly.
161	
162	---
163	
164	## Override rule
165	
166	"Override" means the user picks a different preset action (Defer or Skip in place of Apply, or Apply in place of the agent's recommendation). No inline freeform custom-fix authoring — the walk-through is a decision loop, not a pair-programming surface. A user who wants a variant of the proposed fix picks Skip and hand-edits outside the flow; if they also want the finding tracked, they file a ticket manually. This trade is explicit in v1's scope boundaries.
167	
168	---
169	
170	## State
171	
172	Walk-through state is **in-memory only**. The orchestrator maintains:
173	
174	- An Apply set (finding ids the user picked Apply on)
175	- A decision list (every answered finding with its action and any metadata like `tracker_url` for Deferred or `reason` for Skipped)
176	- The current position in the findings list
177	
178	Nothing is written to disk per-decision. An interrupted walk-through (user cancels the prompt, session compacts, network dies) discards all in-memory state. Defer actions that already executed remain in the tracker — those are external side effects and cannot be rolled back. Apply decisions have not been dispatched yet (they batch at end-of-walk-through), so they are cleanly lost with no code changes.
179	
180	Formal cross-session resumption is out of scope for v1.
181	
182	---
183	
184	## End-of-walk-through dispatch
185	
186	This section covers the run-to-completion path only — every finding has been answered Apply / Defer / Skip / Acknowledge and the loop ended naturally. The `Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest` path exits the walk-through earlier and dispatches its own fixer pass on the union of (accumulated Apply set ∪ remaining undecided findings); see that bullet under "Per-finding routing" above. There is no second dispatch in that branch.
187	
188	When the loop runs to completion, the walk-through hands off to the dispatch phase:
189	
190	1. **Apply set:** spawn one fixer subagent for the full accumulated Apply set. The fixer receives the set as its input queue and applies all changes in one pass against the current working tree. This preserves the existing "one fixer, consistent tree" mechanic and gives the fixer the full set at once to handle inter-fix dependencies (two Applies touching overlapping regions). The existing Step 3 fixer prompt needs a small update to acknowledge this queue may be heterogeneous (`gated_auto` and `manual` mix, not just `safe_auto`) — authored alongside this reference.
191	2. **Defer set:** already executed inline during the walk-through. Nothing to dispatch here.
192	3. **Skip / Acknowledge:** no-op.
193	
194	After dispatch completes, emit the unified completion report described below.
195	
196	---
197	
198	## Unified completion report
199	
200	Every terminal path of Interactive mode emits the same completion report structure. This covers:
201	
202	- Walk-through completed (all findings answered)
203	- Walk-through bailed via `Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest`
204	- Top-level best-judgment (routing option B) completed
205	- Top-level File tickets (routing option C) completed
206	- Zero findings after `safe_auto` (routing question was skipped — the completion summary is a one-line degenerate case of this structure)
207	
208	### Minimum required fields (per R12)
209	
210	- **Per-finding entries:** for every finding the flow touched, a line with — at minimum — title, severity, the action taken (Applied / Deferred / Skipped / Acknowledged), the tracker URL or in-session task reference for Deferred entries, and a one-line reason for Skipped entries (grounded in the finding's confidence or the one-line `why_it_matters` snippet).
211	- **Summary counts by action:** totals per bucket (e.g., `4 applied, 2 deferred, 2 skipped`).
212	- **Failures called out explicitly:** any fix application that failed, any ticket creation that failed (with the reason returned by the tracker). Failures are surfaced above the per-finding list so they are not missed.
213	- **End-of-review verdict:** the existing Stage 6 verdict (Ready to merge / Ready with fixes / Not ready), computed from the residual state after all actions complete.
214	
215	### Coverage section
216	
217	Carry forward the existing Coverage data (suppressed-finding count, residual risks, testing gaps, failed reviewers) and add one new element:
218	
219	- **Framing-enrichment gaps:** count of findings where artifact lookup returned no match (merge-synthesized findings, or failed persona artifact writes). Name the personas contributing those gaps so the data feeds any future persona-upgrade decision. A trail of gaps per run tells the team which persona agents still need attention.
220	
221	### Report ordering
222	
223	The report appears after all execution completes. Ordering inside the report: failures first (above the per-finding list), then per-finding entries grouped by action bucket in the order `Applied / Deferred / Skipped / Acknowledged`, then summary counts, then Coverage, then the verdict.
224	
225	### Zero-findings degenerate case
226	
227	When the routing question was skipped because no `gated_auto` / `manual` findings remained after `safe_auto`, the completion report collapses to its summary-counts + verdict form with one added line — the count of `safe_auto` fixes applied. The summary wording mirrors `SKILL.md` Step 2 Interactive mode's zero-remaining case: the unqualified `All findings resolved` form is only accurate when no advisory or pre-existing findings remain. When advisory and/or pre-existing findings remain in the report, use the qualified form that names what was cleared and names what still remains. Examples:
228	
229	No remaining advisory or pre-existing findings:
230	
231	```
232	All findings resolved — 3 safe_auto fixes applied.
233	
234	Verdict: Ready with fixes.
235	```
236	
237	Advisory and/or pre-existing findings remain in the report:
238	
239	```
240	All actionable findings resolved — 3 safe_auto fixes applied. (2 advisory, 1 pre-existing findings remain in the report.)
241	
242	Verdict: Ready with fixes.
243	```
244	
245	---
246	
247	## Execution posture
248	
249	The walk-through is operationally read-only except for two permitted writes: the in-memory Apply set / decision list (managed by the orchestrator) and the tracker-defer dispatch (external ticket creation, described in `tracker-defer.md`). Persona agents remain strictly read-only. The end-of-walk-through fixer dispatch is the single point where file modifications happen — governed by the existing Step 3 fixer contract in `SKILL.md`.
250
```
</details></tool-use>

## Finding 1 of 5 — P1 Schema version not bumped

`src/db/schemas/settings.ts:19`

**What's wrong**

The `settingsSchema` stays at `version: 0` despite adding a new `tapForgivenessThreshold` property. Existing users with a stored version-0 settings document in IndexedDB will encounter a schema fingerprint mismatch when RxDB initializes the collection, causing a hard error on startup.

**Proposed fix**

Bump schema version to `1` and add a passthrough migration strategy in `create-database.ts` — the field is optional so the identity migration is correct.

**Why it works**

Other schemas in this project (bookmarks, custom_games, saved_game_configs, session_history_index) all carry a version bump plus a `migrationStrategies` entry in `create-database.ts` whenever they gain fields. The learnings-researcher independently confirmed this convention.

---

Apply the version bump + passthrough migration?**

Options:
- **Apply the proposed fix (recommended)** - Bump settings schema to version 1, add identity migration in create-database.ts
- **Defer — file a ticket** - File a GitHub issue to track this without fixing now
- **Skip — don't apply, don't track** - Leave as-is, no action
- **Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest** - Apply fixes the agent can defend for all remaining findings


**Answer:** Apply the proposed fix (recommended)
</details></tool-use>

## Finding 2 of 5 — P2 No reject feedback on micro-drag placement

`src/components/answer-game/useDraggableTile.ts:144`

**What's wrong**

The `onTapFallback` handler calls `placeInNextSlot(tile.id)` but ignores the return value. When a wrong tile is placed via micro-drag forgiveness in `reject` mode, the user gets no shake animation, no wrong sound, no `REJECT_TAP` dispatch, and no `game:evaluate` event — unlike the equivalent `handleClick` path which provides all of these.

**Proposed fix**

Mirror the reject handling from `handleClick` — inspect `placeInNextSlot` result, and when `result.rejected` is true, dispatch `REJECT_TAP`, call `flashBankTileRejectFeedback`, play the wrong sound, and emit `game:evaluate`.

**Why it works**

The `handleClick` path (lines 157-189 of the same file) already implements the full reject feedback contract. The `onTapFallback` path represents the same user intent (place this tile in the next slot) and should produce identical feedback. The drag `onDragStart` already spoke the tile label, so `speakTile` should be skipped to avoid double-speak.

---

- **Apply the proposed fix (recommended)** - Add reject feedback (shake, sound, REJECT_TAP, game:evaluate) to onTapFallback path
- **Defer — file a ticket** - File a GitHub issue to track this without fixing now
- **Skip — don't apply, don't track** - Leave as-is, no action
- **Auto-resolve with best judgment on the rest** - Apply fixes the agent can defend for all remaining findings

## Finding 3 of 5 — P2 Paired options not enforced by TypeScript

`src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:72`

**What's wrong**

`tapForgivenessThreshold` and `onTapFallback` are independently optional in the `UseTouchDragOptions` interface, but the feature only activates when both are set. TypeScript doesn't prevent callers from passing one without the other — the runtime double-check `tapForgivenessThreshold !== undefined && onTapFallbackRef.current` is the only guard.

**Proposed fix**

Replace the two independent optional fields with a discriminated union on `UseTouchDragOptions` so the compiler rejects callers that pass one without the other.

**Why it works**

This eliminates an impossible-state class at compile time. The existing `onHoverZone` / `onHoverBankTile` options in the same interface are also independently optional but they're truly independent — each does something meaningful alone. The tap forgiveness pair is different: neither field has any effect without the other.

## Finding 4 of 5 — P3 Ghost removal duplicated before cleanup

`src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:308`

**What's wrong**

The tap forgiveness path manually nulls `ghostRef` (lines 308-309) before calling `cleanup()`, which itself calls `cleanupGhost()`. This duplicates teardown and creates a brief window outside the `cleanupInProgressRef` re-entrancy guard. The correctness reviewer notes this is "harmless because center already passed the zone check" in the fallthrough case, but it makes the state machine harder to reason about.

**Proposed fix**

Remove the manual ghost teardown and rely solely on `cleanup()` → `cleanupGhost()` which already handles re-entrancy safely.

**Why it works**

Every other terminal path in `onPointerUp` (bank drop, bank-tile drop, zone drop) delegates ghost removal to `cleanup()`. The tap forgiveness path is the only one that tears down the ghost manually first. Unifying the pattern makes the invariant consistent: `cleanup()` is the single point of ghost lifecycle management.

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-02 04:44:08Z)**_

**It's intentional.** The ghost is removed on line 308 *before* `elementsFromPoint` because the ghost is a floating DOM element positioned under the pointer — if it's not removed first, `elementsFromPoint` would return the ghost itself instead of the actual slot zone underneath. This is the exact same pattern used on line 335-337 (with the comment "Remove ghost before elementsFromPoint so it doesn't interfere").

The only side-effect is that when the fallthrough case happens (zone IS found under the pointer, line 319), `ghostRef` is already null by line 332, so `ghostHalfW/ghostHalfH` resolve to 0 — corner expansion uses zero-size corners. This is harmless because center already found the zone (priority 1 succeeds), but it's a subtle implicit coupling.

This isn't naive — it's correct but architecturally fragile. I'll file a GitHub issue to track a cleaner approach.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-05-02 04:44:17Z)**_

<tool-use data-tool-type="shell" data-tool-name="Bash"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **Bash**</summary>
File GitHub issue for ghost cleanup refactor

```bash
gh issue create --title "refactor(touch-drag): save ghost dimensions before tap-forgiveness elementsFromPoint removal" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Context

In `useTouchDrag.ts` `onPointerUp`, the tap-forgiveness block (lines 307-328) removes the ghost element before calling `elementsFromPoint` — this is intentional and correct (the ghost would interfere with hit detection).

However, when the zone-found fallthrough case fires (displacement < threshold AND a zone IS under the pointer), `ghostRef` is already null by the time the normal drop path reads `ghostRef.current?.halfW ?? 0` on line 332. This means corner-expansion uses zero-size corners for that specific case.

## Why it's not a bug today

Center-point detection already found the zone (priority 1 in the drop ladder succeeds), so the zero corner expansion doesn't cause a missed drop. The correctness reviewer confirmed this is harmless.

## Proposed improvement

Save `ghostHalfW` / `ghostHalfH` before the tap-forgiveness removal, or restructure so the ghost is only removed in the `!zoneUnderPointer` branch (and the fallthrough re-enters the normal drop path with the ghost still intact for proper dimension capture).

## References

- PR #299 code review finding
- `src/components/answer-game/useTouchDrag.ts:306-337`
EOF
)"
```

```text
https://github.com/leocaseiro/base-skill/issues/301
```
</details></tool-use>

## Finding 5 of 5 — P3 TDD commit ordering

**Proposed fix**

This is a process observation — the commit history cannot be rewritten without force-push. No code fix applies.

104	): Promise<BaseSkillDatabase> {

111	  const db = await createRxDatabase<BaseSkillDatabase>({
112	    name: `${DB_NAME}-${nanoid()}`,
113	    storage: wrappedValidateAjvStorage({
114	      storage: getRxStorageMemory(),
115	    }),
116	    multiInstance: false,
117	  });
118	  return addBaseSkillCollections(db);
119	}

124	 * Ephemeral in-memory DB for Storybook. Browsers do not allow replacing native
125	 * `indexedDB` with fake-indexeddb, so demos must use memory storage instead.
126	 */
127	export async function createStorybookDatabase(): Promise<BaseSkillDatabase> {
128	  const db = await createRxDatabase<BaseSkillDatabase>({
129	    name: `${STORYBOOK_DB_PREFIX}-${nanoid()}`,
130	    storage: getRxStorageMemory(),
131	    multiInstance: false,
132	  });
133	  const withCols = await addBaseSkillCollections(db);
134	  await checkVersionAndMigrate(withCols);
135	  return withCols;
136	}
137	
138	let productionDbPromise: Promise<BaseSkillDatabase> | undefined;
139	
140	/** Browser-only: opens IndexedDB via Dexie-backed RxStorage and runs migrations. */
141	export async function getOrCreateDatabase(): Promise<BaseSkillDatabase> {
142	  if (typeof indexedDB === 'undefined') {
143	    throw new TypeError(
144	      'getOrCreateDatabase is only available in a browser with IndexedDB',
145	    );
146	  }
147	  productionDbPromise ??= (async () => {
148	    const open = async (): Promise<BaseSkillDatabase> => {
149	      const db = await createRxDatabase<BaseSkillDatabase>({
150	        name: PRODUCTION_DB_NAME,
151	        storage: getRxStorageDexie(),
152	        multiInstance: false,
153	      });
154	      try {
155	        const withCols = await addBaseSkillCollections(db);
156	        await checkVersionAndMigrate(withCols);
157	        return withCols;
158	      } catch (error) {
159	        await db.close().catch(() => {});
160	        throw error;
161	      }
162	    };
163	    // RxDB's parallel migration plumbing intermittently throws DM4 with a
164	    // closed-instance inner error during `addCollections` for users whose
165	    // IndexedDB carries stale legacy schema versions. Recover by wiping
166	    // the broken collection's old storage + meta and retrying. Loop a few
167	    // times in case multiple collections trip the same race in one boot.
168	    const MAX_RECOVERY_ATTEMPTS = 5;
169	    for (let attempt = 0; attempt < MAX_RECOVERY_ATTEMPTS; attempt++) {
170	      try {
171	        return await open();
172	      } catch (error) {
173	        const collection = getMigrationFailureCollection(error);
174	        if (!collection) throw error;
175	        await recoverBrokenMigration(collection);
176	      }
177	    }
178	    return await open();
179	  })();
180	  return productionDbPromise;
181	}
182	
183	export async function destroyTestDatabase(
184	  db?: BaseSkillDatabase,
185	): Promise<void> {
186	  if (db) await db.remove();
187	}

fix(touch-drag): address review findings — schema migration, reject feedback, type safety

- Add reject feedback (shake, sound, REJECT_TAP, game:evaluate) to the

EOF
)"
